title: American Seabed Protection Act
plain_summary: The American Seabed Protection Act stops the government from allowing companies to mine the deep ocean floor and underwater areas near the United States. The only exception is when scientists need to do research. Before any mining can happen, the government must study how it would hurt ocean animals and plants. The government also has to look for other ways to get materials without mining the deep sea. This law protects underwater ecosystems from being damaged by mining.
last_action_text: Sponsor introductory remarks on measure. (CR E86-87)

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the American Seabed Protection Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Prohibition of Certain Mining Activities on Deep Seabed and Outer Continental Shelf
sections.text:
Congress has found several important facts about ocean health and mining. The United Nations says our oceans are in serious danger from climate change, taking resources from them, and pollution. The United Nations created a goal in two thousand fifteen to protect and carefully use oceans, seas, and sea life. Scientists and policy experts around the world agree that mining the deep ocean floor creates major risks to sea life. These risks include killing unique and important sea creatures, creating large clouds of mud and dirt that hurt sea life far from mining areas, making noise that stresses sea animals, possibly contaminating fish that people eat, and likely harming the ocean's ability to store carbon that causes climate change. The United Nations group that protects different kinds of life on Earth encourages countries to make sure deep sea mining impacts are well studied, risks are well understood, and strong rules are in place before allowing such mining. A two thousand twenty-two United Nations report says financing deep sea mining does not follow principles for sustainable ocean business. There is not enough scientific knowledge about the deep sea to fully understand the risks and impacts of deep sea mining.

The government cannot give out licenses, permits, or other approvals for exploring or commercially taking minerals from the deep seabed, even though other laws might allow it. The government also cannot give out licenses, permits, or other approvals for exploring, developing, or producing hard rock minerals from the Outer Continental Shelf (the underwater area that extends from state waters to deep ocean), even though other laws might allow it. However, the government can still give permits for scientific research activities in these areas.

Within ninety days after this law is passed, the Secretary of Commerce must try to make an agreement with the National Academies of Science, Engineering, and Medicine to do a complete study of how mining the deep seabed and Outer Continental Shelf would affect the environment. The Secretary must give a report about the study results to the appropriate Congressional committees.

The study must include several things. It must describe the ecosystems on the deep seabed and Outer Continental Shelf, in the water above these areas, and on underwater mountains and hot water vents. It must assess how mining would impact habitats and species in these areas, including how much area would be affected, how long impacts would last, and whether habitats and species could recover. The study must look at how mining would affect the deep sea's ability to store greenhouse gases. It must examine impacts on people who use the ocean, including commercial and sport fishing, recreation, fish farming, and underwater infrastructure developers. The study must consider impacts on indigenous peoples and cultures connected to sea life and the ocean environment. It must assess how clouds of mud from disturbing the seabed and discharge from mining ships would affect sea life and food chains. The study must estimate greenhouse gas emissions from deep sea mining, including emissions that might result from changing the biology, geology, or chemistry of seabed sediment or water above it. Finally, the study must assess alternatives to using minerals from the deep seabed and Outer Continental Shelf, including better reusing and recycling of these minerals, developing substitute materials with less environmental impact, and reducing environmental impacts of land-based mining.

The definitions section explains what various terms mean. Appropriate Congressional committees include specific House and Senate committees that deal with foreign affairs, natural resources, commerce, science, transportation, energy, and foreign relations. Commercial recovery, deep seabed, development, exploration, Outer Continental Shelf, and production are defined by referring to their meanings in existing laws. The Secretary means the Secretary of Commerce, working through the Administrator of the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration.
